environmental_storytelling.example: Planet Namek stage setting communicates alien conflict; reflects narrative context of Frieza saga
Playable characters (Goku, Vegeta, Gohan, Cell, Androids) represent key story arcs and conflicts from Dragon Ball Z chronology
Unlockable character transformations signal power escalation and story progression points
Story mode progression through themed stages implies temporal and spatial narrative advancement

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Responsive to player input; primary protagonist role
Aggressive AI patterns; high-level threat positioning
Powerful special moves; represents arc-ending antagonist
Mechanical attack patterns; distinct from organic fighter behavior
Complex AI with special transformations; final-boss threat level
Player-controllable; represents narrative shift toward younger generation
Playable character; reflects redemption arc in narrative

notes: Dragon Ball Z: Super Butōden is a 1993 SNES fighting game based on Toriyama's Dragon Ball franchise. The game communicates world-state primarily through character roster selection, stage theming, and battle mechanics. Danger signals derive from health system and positioning mechanics. Faction identity is implicit in character design and narrative roles rather than explicit visual markers like color coding. The split-screen feature with on-screen radar is a signature UI element for tracking spatial relationships during combat. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on anime franchise familiarity—stages like Planet Namek and character selections directly reference narrative arcs from the source material. The game's political situation (multiple factions in conflict: Saiyans, Earthly Defenders, Androids, Galactic Empire) is communicated through which characters are available as opponents in story mode progression rather than explicit visual faction markers.
